name: Enrollment Funnel Forecaster
one_liner: Forecasts cohort enrollment by program and term using 2-3 years of historical admission data, accounting for exam release dates, government scholarship timelines, and program-specific dropout rates.
problem: Enrollment managers at multi-campus HE operators like iPeople and PHINMA use spreadsheets and basic CRM reports to forecast intake. These tools miss education-sector patterns — government scholarship announcement timing, exam result release dates, and campus-specific conversion rates — meaning recruitment spend gets locked in before realistic projections are available.
how_it_works: Ingest 2-3 years of enrollment history by program, term, and funnel stage (inquiry, application, enrollment, retention). Apply seasonal decomposition anchored to education-specific calendar events: exam dates, government funding announcements, school holidays. Output a term-by-program enrollment forecast with confidence intervals, giving enrollment managers a 12-week lead time to adjust recruitment spend and tune program capacity before each intake opens.
scoring_rationale: Enrollment forecasting is a genuine daily pain for HE operators, with clear buyers and accessible historical data. Scores are tempered by functional overlap with ideas 6, 41, and 73, and limited agentic depth — this is primarily a forecasting model, not an orchestrated pipeline.

name: Student Propensity Engine
one_liner: Scores each applicant's likelihood to enroll using structured admission data — GPA, family income proxy, prior school type, distance to campus — so admissions teams can prioritize outreach and avoid over-recruiting thin programs.
problem: PHINMA and iPeople process thousands of applications per intake cycle. Without a systematic propensity model, admissions staff apply the same outreach intensity to everyone, resulting in over-recruited programs and unfilled seats in others. Rising Academies in West Africa need the same logic applied to household demographic data to target community outreach before formal applications exist.
how_it_works: Train a classifier on historical applicant records — GPA, family income indicator, prior school type, campus distance, program choice, test score — against actual enrollment outcome. Score new applicants on enrollment propensity and segment into quintiles. High-propensity applicants get retention-focused outreach; low-propensity get intensive engagement. For Rising Academies, apply the model to neighborhood-level demographic data to generate a household enrollment likelihood map before outreach campaigns begin.
buyer_persona: Admissions Manager or Enrollment Strategy Lead
scoring_rationale: Clear buyer pain and a defined modeling approach, but functionally overlaps with ideas 7, 19, and 74 in the same batch and full list. Data access scores slightly lower because household demographic data for the Rising Academies use case requires non-trivial sourcing outside standard SIS exports.

name: Recruitment ROI Analyzer
one_liner: Measures the incremental enrollment yield and revenue return of each recruitment channel — digital ads, school partnerships, scholarship tiers — using causal inference on historical cohort data.
problem: Marketing and finance teams at iPeople and PHINMA allocate recruitment budgets reactively, shifting spend toward channels that look busy rather than channels that actually convert. There is no model isolating the causal contribution of each channel from confounding factors like program popularity or scholarship generosity, so budget decisions for the next intake are made on gut feel.
ai_technique: Regression & Quantitative Estimation (causal inference on channel contribution)
how_it_works: Analyze cohort-level enrollment data cross-referenced against channel attribution (online ads, school partnership events, scholarship offer level, tuition price). Apply difference-in-differences or propensity score matching to estimate each channel's incremental conversion lift, separating it from program-level demand effects. Model price elasticity curves by program tier. Output a ranked channel ROI table, a budget reallocation recommendation for the next intake, and scenario analysis for new program pricing.
scoring_rationale: Legitimate analytics problem, but the causal inference requirement is methodologically demanding and the data — clean channel attribution by cohort — is rarely available in education CRMs, reducing build feasibility and time-to-value. Buyer authority is split between CMO and CFO, slowing purchase decisions.

name: Appraisal Digest Engine
one_liner: Condenses manager reviews, peer feedback, and professional development portfolios into a structured 1-2 page appraisal summary, aligned to national teaching standards, ready for principal sign-off.
problem: Principals and HR teams at CSPD and PHINMA spend days per appraisal cycle manually collating narrative reviews, self-assessments, and 360 feedback into appraisal documents. Education-specific context — teaching standard alignment, pastoral observation notes, curriculum area expertise — gets flattened or lost in generic HRIS summary fields, reducing appraisal quality and increasing the time cost per teacher.
how_it_works: Ingest appraisal source documents: manager narrative review, 360 feedback responses, self-assessment, PD portfolio, and certification status. Generate a standardized 1-2 page executive summary structured around national teaching standards (e.g., NESA for Australian schools, DepEd competencies for the Philippines). Flag evidence gaps and surface domain-specific themes — pastoral care incidents, curriculum leadership, subject specialist performance. Output a draft digest for principal review, reducing per-teacher processing time from hours to minutes.
scoring_rationale: High feasibility and fast time-to-value — document summarization with domain-specific structure is well within current LLM capabilities and appraisal documents are readily accessible. Defensibility is low because the core technique (LLM summarization with a structured prompt) is replicable; differentiation comes from education ontology depth and workflow integration.

name: Workforce Data Unifier
one_liner: Normalizes teacher records across payroll, HRIS, national registration, and appraisal systems into a single canonical schema that supports workforce analytics without point-to-point integrations.
problem: Multi-site operators like CSPD and PHINMA run payroll on one platform, HRIS on another, national teacher registration through a government portal, and appraisal tracking in spreadsheets. No system holds a complete view of a teacher's registration status, subject area, contract type, professional learning hours, and compensation. Education-specific attributes — vocational vs. academic subject split, multi-site contract apportionment — are not handled by generic HR integration tools.
ai_technique: Data Transformation & Structuring (education HR schema mapping)
how_it_works: Define a canonical education HR schema covering registration status, teaching subject and level, contract type (permanent, casual, fixed-term), PD hours by category, and compensation components. Build ETL pipelines mapping each source system to this schema, with education-specific transformation rules: reconcile vocational vs. academic subject codes, handle teachers with split contracts across campuses, flag registration expiry mismatches. Output: a unified staff database refreshed nightly that feeds workforce analytics without requiring source system changes.
buyer_persona: Director of IT or Head of HR
scoring_rationale: Real integration pain exists, but this is fundamentally a data engineering problem — ETL pipelines and schema mapping — not an agentic AI problem. Time-to-value is long because each source system requires bespoke connectors, and the buyer is split between IT and HR, neither of whom has sole budget authority.

name: Teacher Attrition Forecaster
one_liner: Predicts teacher attrition by cohort and subject area over the next 2-3 academic terms, using contract milestones, certification expiry dates, and seasonal departure patterns to produce a forward hiring plan.
problem: Rising Academies and PHINMA face persistent teacher retention pressure, with attrition concentrated at predictable points — end-of-year contract decisions, certification renewal deadlines, and post-exam-season departures. HR teams discover vacancies too late to recruit without disrupting classes, and legacy HRIS cannot produce a forward-looking staffing gap analysis by subject area and campus.
how_it_works: Ingest teacher roster records: contract type, subject area, tenure, certification expiry, school assignment, and historical departure dates. Model attrition as a combination of seasonal patterns (end-of-year peaks) and event-driven signals (contracts expiring within 90 days, certifications lapsing). Forecast hiring demand for the next 2-3 terms by subject and campus, with confidence intervals. Output a monthly hiring plan, individual-level risk alerts for teachers with imminent contract or certification triggers, and recommended recruitment lead-time by subject scarcity.
buyer_persona: Chief People Officer or Talent Manager
scoring_rationale: Teacher attrition is a severe operational problem in sub-Saharan Africa and SE Asian affordable HE — a missed hire can mean a class with no teacher for weeks. Data is accessible from contract and registration records, and a working v1 model can be validated within one academic term. Duplicate of idea 3 but with stronger emphasis on forward hiring planning.

name: Professional Development Scheduler
one_liner: Generates an optimized annual PD schedule for distributed school networks, assigning courses to dates and sites while respecting mandatory PD hours, award conditions, and relief teacher availability constraints.
problem: Rising Academies and School for Life operate 50+ distributed campuses. Coordinating professional development across sites is done manually in spreadsheets, creating scheduling conflicts, missed mandatory hours, and unnecessary relief teacher costs. There is no tool that accounts simultaneously for award compliance (mandatory PD days per year), program certification requirements (specific courses per role), and the operational constraint of maintaining class coverage during training days.
how_it_works: Model PD budget, teacher availability by site and subject area, award compliance rules (minimum PD hours per role per year), program certification requirements (mandatory courses), and relief teacher pool capacity. Solve as a constraint satisfaction problem: assign each course to a date and campus combination that maximizes attendance, stays within budget, and maintains required class coverage. Output an annual PD calendar by campus, a budget allocation by course type, and a relief cover plan flagging dates where supply is tight.
buyer_persona: Director of Professional Learning or Operations Manager
scoring_rationale: The scheduling constraint problem is technically interesting but the buyer (Director of Professional Learning) often lacks independent budget authority, and the pain is less acute than attrition or compliance risks. An exact duplicate of idea 4 by name and concept.

name: Teacher Risk Intervention Queue
one_liner: Ranks teachers monthly on three risk dimensions — flight risk, performance risk, and certification lapse — so HR can intervene before a departure, a class quality problem, or a compliance breach occurs.
problem: PHINMA's distributed workforce of 1,000+ teachers means HR cannot monitor individual risk manually. High performers in remote postings leave without warning. Teachers with expiring TESDA or PRC certifications continue in role past compliance deadlines. Staff in hard-to-fill subject areas resign without triggering a retention response. These are discovered only after the fact.
how_it_works: Build a composite risk model across three dimensions: flight risk (tenure, remote posting, role-market wage gap, prior cohort departure patterns), performance risk (appraisal scores, student outcome trends, absence frequency), and compliance risk (certification expiry, registration renewal status). Score each teacher monthly and surface the top-risk cohort as a prioritized intervention queue. Each entry includes the dominant risk factor and a recommended action — targeted retention conversation, role adjustment, PD assignment, or urgent compliance follow-up.
buyer_persona: Head of HR or Talent Development Manager
scoring_rationale: Multi-dimensional risk scoring with intervention routing is a strong agentic use case — decomposing flight, performance, and compliance risk requires orchestrated data retrieval and reasoning, not just a lookup. Buyer clarity and problem severity are high; the main constraint is assembling clean input data across fragmented HR systems.

name: Grant Compliance Assistant
one_liner: Answers compliance and reporting questions — funding eligibility, expenditure rules, reporting deadlines — by searching across an institution's uploaded grant agreements, donor policies, and budget circulars.
problem: Finance teams at PHINMA, Rising Academies, and Acasus-supported institutions manage funding from multiple sources with conflicting conditions: government grants with restricted expenditure categories, World Bank loans with procurement requirements, philanthropic grants with earmarking rules. The answers to compliance questions are buried in lengthy PDF agreements. Knowledge is concentrated in one or two staff members who become bottlenecks and single points of failure.
how_it_works: Upload all institutional funding documents — grant agreements, donor policy manuals, government funding circulars, audit requirement schedules — to a vector store. When a finance officer asks 'Can we use the CHED grant for staff overtime?' or 'What is the reporting deadline for the AfDB disbursement?', retrieve the relevant clauses and generate a cited answer with document reference and page number. Flag ambiguous clauses that require legal or donor clarification. Output a searchable compliance Q&A log and a summary of all active reporting deadlines by funder.
scoring_rationale: Near-perfect profile for a RAG product: documents exist and are uploadable, the buyer (CFO or Grants Manager) has clear authority and budget, and ROI is visible within days of deployment. Closest to GrantLens of any batch-2 idea — functionally this is a core GrantLens feature, not a standalone product.

name: Budget Variance Synthesizer
one_liner: Transforms monthly budget-vs-actual reports across programs and cost centers into a one-page CFO narrative, automatically flagging material variances and their likely causes.
problem: Finance teams at PHINMA and Rising Academies produce detailed variance reports monthly — actuals vs. budget across 20-50 cost centers and multiple funding sources. Translating this into a coherent executive summary for the CFO and board is done manually, takes several hours per cycle, and produces inconsistent write-ups depending on who drafts them.
how_it_works: Ingest monthly budget-vs-actual data by program, department, and funding source (CSV or ERP export). Identify material variances automatically using configurable thresholds (e.g., >10% or >USD 50k). For each material variance, generate a plain-language explanation drawing on prior month narrative and known calendar context (e.g., delayed grant disbursement, enrollment shortfall, deferred capex). Output a one-page executive summary with the top five variances by impact, their explanations, and any required board or funder notification.
scoring_rationale: Very fast to build and fast to show value — CSV in, narrative out. But problem severity is moderate (finance teams find this tedious, not crisis-level) and defensibility is low because summarizing tabular data is a thin moat. Overlaps significantly with ideas 27 and 37.

name: Fee Default Risk Predictor
one_liner: Scores enrolled students monthly on tuition default risk using payment history, course performance, and financial aid status, giving student services teams a prioritized intervention list before students stop attending.
problem: PHINMA and iPeople serve first-generation, lower-income students for whom fee default is the primary path to dropout. Institutions currently discover default only when a student stops attending — by which point re-engagement is difficult. Earlier identification (4-6 weeks before default) would allow targeted interventions: payment plan adjustment, emergency bursary, or scholarship extension. No existing SIS module provides this forward-looking signal.
how_it_works: Build a monthly default risk model on enrolled students using: prior fee payment timing patterns, current academic standing, changes in enrolment load (partial withdrawal signals), financial aid type and remaining balance, and family income proxy. Score each student and segment into intervention tiers — high-risk students get flagged for proactive contact by student services within the same week. Unlike credit risk models, the output is a support referral, not a service cutoff. Include a recommended intervention type (payment plan, emergency aid, scholarship review) based on the student's financial profile.
buyer_persona: Chief Student Services Officer or Registrar
scoring_rationale: Strong problem severity — fee default leading to dropout is a daily operational crisis in affordable HE. Data exists in SIS and financial records, and a working model can be validated against one term's cohort within the first deployment cycle. Overlaps with idea 44 and partially with idea 28.

name: Campus Capital Planner
one_liner: Recommends capex allocation across campuses by optimizing for facility utilization targets and government funding eligibility rules, replacing manual spreadsheet-based budget allocation.
problem: CFOs at PHINMA, iPeople, and CSPD allocate capital budgets across 10-30 campuses without a systematic model. Government funding rules restrict which expenditure categories are eligible, enrollment varies significantly by campus, and facility utilization directly affects funding justification. The result is ad-hoc allocation that leaves some campuses over-invested relative to enrollment and others with maintenance backlogs that risk funding compliance.
how_it_works: Model the capex budget by funding source, with government-imposed eligibility rules for each category (construction, equipment, IT, maintenance). Input campus enrollment actuals and capacity utilization targets. Run a constrained optimization to allocate budget across campuses: maximizing utilization improvement per dollar, complying with government expenditure restrictions, and prioritizing campuses with the highest maintenance risk scores. Output a campus-by-category budget recommendation, a utilization forecast post-investment, and a compliance check against each funding source's rules.
scoring_rationale: Capital planning is a real need but runs on annual cycles, making time-to-value long and ROI hard to demonstrate within a school term. The constrained optimization problem is technically sound but requires clean facility utilization and government funding rule data that is often not digitized.

name: Multi-Source Budget Configuration Engine
one_liner: Finds a feasible budget allocation that satisfies simultaneous constraints: government grant rules, donor earmarking, internal cross-subsidy targets, and minimum program viability thresholds.
problem: CFOs at PHINMA and Acasus-supported institutions must balance four conflicting constraint sets in one budget: government grants with restricted/unrestricted expenditure categories, donor grants with earmarked program allocations, internal cross-subsidy targets (affordable programs funded by premium program surpluses), and minimum viability floors for each program. Manual spreadsheet iteration cannot guarantee feasibility or surface the right trade-offs for board discussion.
how_it_works: Encode all active constraints: government funding rules by category, donor conditions and earmarking by grant, cross-subsidy targets, and program viability floors. Run a constraint solver over the budget input to find feasible allocations. If no feasible solution exists, identify which constraint sets are in conflict and by how much. Enable scenario analysis — 'What if we reduce the affordable program subsidy by 5%?' — and show the downstream impact on each constraint. Output a recommended allocation with a constraint compliance summary and a trade-off brief for the Finance Committee.
buyer_persona: CFO or Board Finance Committee Chair
scoring_rationale: Genuinely novel — no existing SaaS tool solves multi-constraint education budget configuration. Defensibility is higher because the constraint model embeds deep domain knowledge. But time-to-value is long (annual budget cycle) and encoding all constraints requires significant client-side data work before the model can run.

name: Funder Requirement Navigator
one_liner: Answers project eligibility and procurement questions by searching across uploaded funder agreements — World Bank, African Development Bank, government grants — and returning cited answers from the source documents.
problem: Project managers at Acasus-supported institutions, Rising Academies, and School for Life plan capital projects under multilateral funder agreements that run to hundreds of pages. Determining whether a specific expenditure is eligible, what the procurement approval threshold is, or when a disbursement report is due requires reading the right section of the right agreement. This is done manually, slowly, and inconsistently across team members.
how_it_works: Upload funder agreements and operational manuals (World Bank OM, AfDB procurement guidelines, bilateral government grant conditions) into a vector store. Enable project managers to query: 'Is solar panel installation eligible under the FCDO grant?' 'What is the procurement threshold for equipment under the World Bank loan?' Return cited answers with clause references and page numbers. Auto-extract and maintain a structured requirements register: eligibility rules, procurement thresholds, reporting deadlines, and approval authorities by funder and project.
buyer_persona: Project Manager or Director of Planning
scoring_rationale: Functionally very close to the Grant Compliance Assistant — both are RAG systems over funding documents answering eligibility questions. The capital project framing (funder agreements vs. general grant conditions) is a nuance rather than a distinct product. High build feasibility and immediate time-to-value once documents are uploaded.

name: Funder Acquittal Synthesizer
one_liner: Reads funder acquittal reports and audit findings and produces a two-page executive brief that lists compliance findings by severity, required remediation actions, and outstanding deadlines.
problem: CFOs and Audit Committees at Rising Academies, School for Life, and Acasus-supported institutions receive audit reports from external auditors and development funders that identify compliance findings across 50-200 pages. Translating these into actionable leadership briefs is done manually by finance staff who must read the full report, categorize findings, and draft a summary — often taking two or more days per report cycle.
how_it_works: Ingest funder acquittal reports, external audit findings, and management response documents. Extract all findings and classify by risk rating (critical, major, minor) and finding type (financial, governance, procurement, programmatic). Map each finding to a required remediation action and due date from the auditor's recommendation. Generate a two-page executive brief with a findings register (ranked by risk), remediation timeline, and a summary of resource implications. Output includes an issue tracker pre-populated with responsible parties and due dates for follow-up.
scoring_rationale: Fast to build and demonstrates value within the first audit cycle. The buyer (CFO or Audit Committee Chair) is well-defined and the documents are readily available. Problem severity is moderate — audit synthesis is painful but infrequent (typically 1-2 cycles per year), limiting recurring value relative to daily workflow tools.

name: Capital Fundraising Pipeline
one_liner: Tracks capital project funding gaps, maps them to eligible funders based on project type and geography, and recommends the next funder to engage based on relationship strength and strategic fit.
problem: Rising Academies and School for Life pursue expansion capital from a mix of government grants, development banks, and philanthropic trusts. Without a systematic tracker, the team lacks a clear view of which capital projects have unfunded gaps, which existing funder relationships could fill them, and which new funder prospects should be prioritized. Funding pursuit is reactive and relationship-driven rather than strategically sequenced.
how_it_works: Maintain a capital project registry with funding requirements, committed funding, and remaining gap by project. Build a funder eligibility matrix: for each funder, encode their sector focus, eligible countries, instrument type (grant, concessional loan, equity), and minimum/maximum project size. As funding gaps emerge, score the funder portfolio by fit on three dimensions: relationship strength (prior grants, active engagement), strategic alignment (focus country, sector, instrument), and eligibility match. Output a ranked funder engagement list by project gap, with recommended outreach sequencing and context for each approach.
buyer_persona: Chief Development Officer or Fundraising Manager
scoring_rationale: Genuine gap in the market — no dedicated tool exists for education NGO capital fundraising pipeline management. But the buyer role (Chief Development Officer) is uncommon and often underfunded in NGO contexts, and the funder eligibility data needed to power recommendations requires significant manual curation to set up.

name: Capital Project Risk Monitor
one_liner: Monitors active capital projects for cost overrun trajectory, schedule slip, and funder compliance breaches, issuing early warnings before thresholds trigger funder sanctions.
problem: Acasus-supported institutions and Rising Academies run concurrent capital projects with tight funder timelines and fixed budgets. Cost overruns and schedule slippage are common but are typically identified only at monthly reporting — too late for low-cost remediation. Procurement rule breaches (e.g., equipment purchased outside approved thresholds) can trigger funder sanctions or full claw-back of disbursed funds.
how_it_works: Ingest capital project records: approved budget by category, spend-to-date, committed purchase orders, schedule baseline, milestone dates, and funder procurement rules. Track cost burn rate and schedule completion percentage against plan. Flag early warning signals when: spend trajectory is on course to exceed budget by >10% by project end; schedule completion is running >4 weeks behind baseline; procurement activities approach or exceed funder approval thresholds. Score each project's overall compliance risk on a weekly basis. Output a project health dashboard with traffic-light indicators, recommended remediation actions, and draft funder notification text for critical findings.
buyer_persona: Chief Operations Officer or Project Director
scoring_rationale: Funder claw-back risk makes cost overrun and compliance breaches acutely painful — this problem has real financial consequence, not just operational inconvenience. Agentic suitability is solid: the monitor needs to decompose budget trajectory, schedule, and compliance signals across multiple projects and generate differentiated alerts. Data access scores lower because project financial data is often in ERP systems requiring integration effort.
